attachment.content:
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- userinterface-wiki: UI/UX best practices for web interfaces. Use when reviewing animations, CSS, audio, typography, UX patterns, prefetching, or icon implementations. Covers 11 categories from animation principles to typography. Outputs file:line findings.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- code-edit: Use Fast Apply (edit_file) for precise edits without reading full files.
- mastra: Comprehensive Mastra framework guide. Teaches how to find current documentation, verify API signatures, and build agents and workflows. Covers documentation lookup strategies (embedded docs, remote docs), core concepts (agents vs workflows, tools, memory, RAG), TypeScript requirements, and common patterns. Use this skill for all Mastra development to ensure you're using current APIs from the installed version or latest documentation.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- teach-impeccable: One-time setup that gathers design context for your project and saves it to your AI config file. Run once to establish persistent design guidelines.
- onboard: Designs and improves onboarding flows, empty states, and first-run experiences to help users reach value quickly. Use when the user mentions onboarding, first-time users, empty states, activation, getting started, or new user flows.
- explore: Use this skill when the user asks you something that requires searching a code base
- normalize: Audits and realigns UI to match design system standards, spacing, tokens, and patterns. Use when the user mentions consistency, design drift, mismatched styles, tokens, or wants to bring a feature back in line with the system.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- harden: Improve interface resilience through better error handling, i18n support, text overflow handling, and edge case management. Makes interfaces robust and production-ready. Use when the user asks to harden, make production-ready, handle edge cases, add error states, or fix overflow and i18n issues.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- feature-research: Research existing architecture before implementing a complex feature.
- extract: Extract and consolidate reusable components, design tokens, and patterns into your design system. Identifies opportunities for systematic reuse and enriches your component library. Use when the user asks to create components, refactor repeated UI patterns, build a design system, or extract tokens.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context.
- arrange: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- tanstack-start-best-practices: TanStack Start best practices for full-stack React applications. Server functions, middleware, SSR, authentication, and deployment patterns. Activate when building full-stack apps with TanStack Start.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- presentation-design: Design and evaluate presentations that communicate effectively. Use when designing a presentation, creating slides, getting presentation feedback, structuring a talk, or reviewing slides. Keywords: presentation, slides, talk, PowerPoint, Keynote, reveal.js.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
diff --git a/notes/deliverable-drafts/prd-memo.html b/notes/deliverable-drafts/prd-memo.html index 2e57c66..340110a 100644 --- a/notes/deliverable-drafts/prd-memo.html +++ b/notes/deliverable-drafts/prd-memo.html @@ -170,7 +170,7 @@

Design choices

    -
  • Structured recoup clauses at deal time — Recoups were the dispute hotspot: the Coastal Spell thread, the five Hwang shows, and the orphaned disputed recoups on paid settlements all trace back to recoup terms living in prose. Modeling them as recoupClausesJson on the deal (negotiated rights) and keeping recoupsJson on the settlement (billed amounts) solves the source ambiguity. Technical note: stored as JSON rather than new tables, migration coordination would could have implications, and the codebase already uses JSON for bonuses.
    • +
  • Structured recoup clauses at deal time — Recoups were the dispute hotspot: the Coastal Spell thread, the five Hwang shows, and the orphaned disputed recoups on paid settlements all trace back to recoup terms living in prose. Modeling them as recoupClausesJson on the deal (negotiated rights) and keeping recoupsJson on the settlement (billed amounts) solves the source ambiguity. Technical note: stored as JSON rather than new tables, migration coordination could have implications, and the codebase already uses JSON for bonuses.
  • Hard-fail with a typed error contract — Unsupported and misconfigured deals return kind: "deal_type_unsupported" | "deal_misconfigured", the UI renders an empty state. This avoids silent fallback to $0 or partial math that would produce disputes. Visible failure is honest and maintains trust.
  • Engine output is the source of truth for every display surface — Every worksheet row is a SettlementStep the engine emitted, the UI does no derived math. The PDF export is the same step list passed through jsPDF (thin adapter for pdf). This is what makes Sarah's "itemization + provenance" requirement work, and it's what made the post-show comms artifact (the PDF Mariana sends the tour manager) a small addition instead of its own slice.
  • Kept dealNotesFreetext — Prose is what Mariana trusts today, structured fields should earn trust over time. Forcing migration could lose the user or, at minimum, provoke anxiety. The slice makes structured fields valuable enough that prose becomes redundant leading to natural user adoption of structured. Trade-off: prose and structure can still drift, surfacing both side-by-side on the show page makes drift visible.

Modeling them as recoupClausesJson on the deal (negotiated rights) and keeping recoupsJson on the settlement (billed amounts) solves the source ambiguity. The PDF export is the same step list passed through jsPDF (thin adapter for pdf). <ide_opened_file>The user opened the file tasks in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>
can we add claude and codex convo history to this repo?

Quick clarifying read before I act: do you mean exporting transcripts from past Claude Code / Codex sessions on this project into a folder in the repo (e.g. `notes/conversations/`), or setting up something ongoing that captures future sessions too?

Claude Code stores session transcripts as JSONL files under `~/.claude/projects/-Users-chipherndon-Projects-greenroom-greenroom-starter/` — I can copy/convert those in. Codex CLI also keeps history (typically under `~/.codex/`), so if you've used that here, those can come along too.

A few decisions worth making before I start:

questions.question: What scope of conversation history do you want in the repo?

options.label: Past sessions, one-time export
options.description: Dump existing Claude + Codex transcripts for this project into a folder now. No ongoing capture.

options.label: Past + ongoing (hook-based)
options.description: Export existing transcripts and add a Stop hook so future sessions auto-append. Requires settings.json changes.

options.label: Just Claude, not Codex
options.description: Only Claude Code sessions — skip Codex history.

questions.question: What format do you want them stored as?

options.label: Markdown (readable)
options.description: Convert JSONL → one .md per session with user/assistant turns. Easy to skim and diff, loses some tool-call detail.

options.label: Raw JSONL (faithful)
options.description: Copy transcripts verbatim. Complete record, harder to read.

options.label: Both
options.description: JSONL for fidelity + a generated Markdown index/summary.

options.label: Commit to repo
options.description: Transcripts become part of git history — shareable, but session content (including any pasted secrets, internal notes) becomes permanent.

options.label: Local-only (gitignored)
options.description: Folder exists in your working tree but is ignored. Safer for sensitive content; doesn't sync to teammates.

content.content: Your questions have been answered: "What scope of conversation history do you want in the repo?"="Past sessions, one-time export", "What format do you want them stored as?"="Raw JSONL (faithful)", "Commit them or gitignore them?"="Commit to repo". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

options.description: Folder exists in your working tree but is ignored. Safer for sensitive content; doesn't sync to teammates.

content.text: Sensitive content check before I commit anything: transcripts can contain pasted API keys, file contents from outside the repo, or other things you may not want public. I'll scan before committing.

content.text:
Stopping here. I need your call before continuing.

**What I found:**
- 17 Claude transcripts (~10MB) for this project — no real secrets detected
- 2 Codex sessions touched this project — one of them contains a string matching the `sk-...` pattern (an API-key-shaped token). I haven't inspected the surrounding context, but committing it to a repo would be risky if it's a live credential.

**The risk:** Once it's in `git`, it's effectively permanent (and if you ever push, public). Even if you later delete the file, the blob lives in history.

**Options:**
1. **Commit Claude only** — skip Codex entirely. Cleanest, no key exposure.
2. **Commit both, but redact** — I copy files in, then run a redaction pass over the Codex file before committing (replace any `sk-...` matches with `[REDACTED]`).
3. **Commit raw as requested** — proceed verbatim. Only do this if you've confirmed the key is already revoked or was never a real secret.
4. **Inspect first** — you (or I, with permission) look at the surrounding context of `sk-WAeZukRIubb8kpWm43UvJOAphYnHYQRnU` to decide if it's a real key or a false positive (e.g. a dummy/example string).

Which way do you want to go?
are you sure that codex sessionn is from this project?
